title: Node File Services, Object Storage, and CDN Architecture · Narrowing Workshop
summary: A TypeScript pairing room incrementally builds Node storage-architecture decision record, validates one changed runtime input, and keeps earlier HTTP, accessibility, security, stream, AbortSignal, and resource-budget rules active. Planned artifact: Build a complete first version of Node storage-architecture decision record while every earlier competency remains active. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidence defense.
objectives: In a strict Node 24 and TypeScript 7 service, compare local filesystems, shared filesystems, object storage, databases, origin services, and CDNs by interface, consistency, scale, trust, and operations.
In a strict Node 24 and TypeScript 7 service, separate control-plane metadata and authorization from high-volume data transfer while preserving one auditable decision.

In a strict Node 24 and TypeScript 7 service, choose server upload, direct upload, redirect, proxy, signed edge delivery, or public delivery from threat, transformation, audit, and performance evidence.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

steps.title: S3, CloudFront, and TypeScript Commit to the mission model · Workshop Filesystem Object Cdn
steps.instruction:
workshop case — A workshop TypeScript file-delivery team handles case 4320 during a private media entitlement change. Build deterministic TypeScript evidence to select a typed control and data plane from trust, transformation, and volume evidence; keep AbortSignal and stream cleanup under one named owner; then double concurrent Upload instances and preserve aggregate memory and worker budgets. Browser code uses pure values and state transitions only. Node and AWS SDK packages, networks, S3, CloudFront, KMS, IAM, WAF, PostgreSQL, credentials, host state, handle and stream checks, load, faults, restore, billing, and production behavior require explicit authorized transfer gates. Competency probe: In a strict Node 24 and TypeScript 7 service, compare local filesystems, shared filesystems, object storage, databases, origin services, and CDNs by interface, consistency, scale, trust, and operations.

What must be established before implementation?

Evidence target: S3, CloudFront, and TypeScript Commit to the mission model · Workshop Filesystem Object Cdn.
steps.why: The decision supplies observable evidence for in a strict node 24 and typescript 7 service, compare local filesystems, shared filesystems, object storage, databases, origin services, and cdns by interface, consistency, scale, trust, and operations. For S3, CloudFront, and TypeScript Commit to the mission model · Workshop Filesystem Object Cdn, keep Filesystem Object Cdn connected to executable TypeScript storage and delivery decision evidence.

Browser code uses pure values and state transitions only. Node and AWS SDK packages, networks, S3, CloudFront, KMS, IAM, WAF, PostgreSQL, credentials, host state, handle and stream checks, load, faults, restore, billing, and production behavior require explicit authorized transfer gates. steps.title: S3, CloudFront, and TypeScript Extend the cumulative artifact · Workshop Cost Failure Model
steps.instruction:
Append a compile-ready pure-TypeScript function headed "// Evidence: storage-ts-cost-failure-model-871d0h" that uses case 3205 to select a typed control and data plane from trust, transformation, and volume evidence. Guided rehearsal: predict the branch, make one bounded increment, and inspect peer-readable changed output. Return observable changed-case evidence. Browser code must not import Node or AWS SDK packages, open sockets, contact S3, CloudFront, KMS, IAM, WAF, PostgreSQL, networks, or external effects, read host state, execute host commands, or use credentials; verify those boundaries later with the TypeScript 7.0.2 native compiler and the @typescript/typescript6 package 6.0.2 compiler that reports 6.0.3, Node 24.18.0, AWS SDK v3.1087.0, controlled AWS, stream and handle, load, fault, security, cost, restore, and production gates.
